---
title: "The shared solvers: small systems, safe steps, and big covariances"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
Strip away the statistics and every fit in this suite reduces to a handful of
numerical jobs. Two of them recur in *every* chapter: **solve a small dense
system** $A\beta = c$ where $A$ is $p\times p$ (the normal equations), and
**choose a few scalars** — variances, smoothing strengths — by a cheap **Newton**
iteration. Two further tricks keep the *big* $N\times N$ observation covariance
from ever being formed: the **Woodbury identity** (correct a cheap inverse with a
small rank-$q$ fix) and **spectral diagonalisation** (one eigendecomposition turns
the problem into independent 1-D coordinates). The single new idea of this page:
*all four are hand-rolled in `nitrix`* — no vendor GPU solver is ever called —
because the cuSOLVER custom call is broken on the target accelerators. This page
is the shared substrate the model chapters link to instead of re-deriving the
numerics each time.
:::

This is a *machinery* page, not a rung on [the ladder](index.qmd). The model
chapters — [GLM](glm.qmd), [LME](lme.qmd), [GAM](gam.qmd), [GLMM](glmm.qmd) — each
point here for the four numerical primitives they all share. Read it once and the
later chapters get shorter: when [the GLM chapter](glm.qmd#sec-irls) says "solve
the penalised normal equations" or [the LME chapter](lme.qmd) says "the FaST-LMM
trick", the *how* lives here.

## Four jobs, one accelerator

It is worth seeing, up front, that the diversity of models in this suite hides a
very small numerical core. A linear model, a logistic regression, a smoothing
spline, a mixed model — fit any of them and you will, in the inner loop, be doing
one of exactly four things:

1. **Solve a small dense SPD system** $A\beta = c$, with $A$ a $p\times p$
   symmetric positive-definite matrix (the normal equations, possibly weighted
   and penalised). Section [§2](#sec-cholesky).
2. **Minimise a small smooth objective** in a few scalars (log-variances,
   smoothing parameters) by Newton's method. Section [§3](#sec-newton).
3. **Invert a giant $N\times N$ covariance** $V_y = ZGZ^{\top}+R$ without ever
   forming it — by the Woodbury identity. Section [§4](#sec-woodbury).
4. **Diagonalise once** so a whole sweep of trial variances is cheap — the
   spectral / FaST-LMM trick. Section [§5](#sec-spectral).

A word on the hardware constraint that shapes all four, because it is unusual and
it explains design choices that would otherwise look perverse. The natural way to
solve $A\beta=c$ on a GPU is to call the vendor library: in JAX that is
`jnp.linalg.solve` / `jnp.linalg.cholesky` / `jnp.linalg.eigh`, which lower to
**cuSOLVER** custom calls (`potrf` for Cholesky, `syevd` for the symmetric
eigendecomposition, `getrf` for LU). On the development accelerator (an NVIDIA L4)
the cuSOLVER handle pool is dead — the *first* cuSOLVER routine in a process fails
to create a handle, so those calls raise rather than compute. Only cuBLAS
(matrix multiply and `triangular_solve`/`trsm`) and the elementwise surface work.
This is documented in the source itself (see the module docstring of
`linalg/_smalllinalg.py`).

The consequence is a hard rule that runs through everything below: **issue no
cuSOLVER custom call at any size**. Where a textbook would call a library Cholesky
or eig, `nitrix` ships a hand-rolled version built from matmul, `sqrt`, division,
and `triangular_solve` — all of which *do* work, and all of which are
differentiable (so the whole fit can be run under `jax.grad`, `jit`, and `vmap`
across a million voxels at once). The one exception is the *single, shared*
eigendecomposition of [§5](#sec-spectral), which is computed once per dataset
through a robust `safe_eigh` that falls back to the CPU if cuSOLVER is unavailable
— it is outside the per-voxel hot path, so it can afford to.

A motivating example to carry through. Picture a voxelwise fMRI analysis: at each
of $V\approx 10^5$–$10^6$ voxels you have $N$ observations and want to fit the
same model — say cortical activation as a function of a task regressor, with a
random intercept per subject. That is one tiny fit, `vmap`-ed a million times. The
*size* of each fit is small ($p$ a handful, $N$ a few dozen to a few hundred); the
*number* of fits is enormous. Every choice below is made to keep the per-fit work
tiny, branch-free, and on the accelerator.

## The small dense solve {#sec-cholesky}

### The system, and why not to invert

Start from the job. Fitting a linear model by least squares means solving the
**normal equations** (derived in [the GLM chapter](glm.qmd#sec-irls)); in the
weighted, penalised form that the whole suite actually uses they read

$$
\underbrace{(X^{\top} W X + S)}_{A}\,\beta = \underbrace{X^{\top} W z}_{c},
$$ {#eq-normal}

with $W$ a diagonal matrix of working weights, $z$ a working response, and $S$ the
**penalty matrix** (roughness or precision; zero for an ordinary GLM, a smoothing
penalty for a [GAM](gam.qmd#sec-penalty)). The matrix $A = X^{\top} W X + S$ is
$p\times p$, **symmetric** (it is a Gram matrix plus a symmetric penalty), and
**positive-definite** (as long as $X$ has full column rank and $W\succ 0$).
Symmetric positive-definite — SPD — is the friendliest class of matrix there is,
and the algorithm below earns its keep by exploiting exactly that.

The naive thing a newcomer reaches for is to compute the inverse $A^{-1}$ and form
$\beta = A^{-1} c$. Resist it, for two reasons. First, it is **wasteful**:
computing the full inverse does roughly three times the arithmetic of just solving
for the one $\beta$ you want, and you usually do not need the other entries of
$A^{-1}$. Second — and this matters more — forming $A^{-1}$ and multiplying is
**less numerically stable** than solving the system directly; the explicit inverse
amplifies rounding error in a way a direct solve does not. The rule of thumb every
numerical analyst internalises: *never invert when you can solve.*

So how do we solve an SPD system without inverting? With its square root.

### Cholesky: the square root of an SPD matrix

Here is the idea you could have had yourself. Gaussian elimination factors any
matrix into a lower-triangular times an upper-triangular piece, $A = LU$.
Triangular systems are trivial to solve — you read off one unknown at a time. So
elimination *turns a hard solve into two easy ones*. Now add the one fact that an
SPD matrix gives you for free: it is symmetric, so the lower and upper factors are
not independent — the upper one is just the transpose of the lower. The whole
factorisation collapses to a single triangular factor:

$$
A = L L^{\top}, \qquad L \text{ lower-triangular with } L_{ii} > 0.
$$ {#eq-chol}

This $L$ is the **Cholesky factor** — the matrix "square root" of $A$ in the sense
that $L L^{\top} = A$, exactly the way $\sqrt{a}\,\sqrt{a} = a$ for a positive
scalar. (That an SPD matrix *has* such a factor, and a unique one with positive
diagonal, is the content of the Cholesky theorem; positive-definiteness is what
guarantees every quantity you take a square root of below stays positive.)

You can read the recurrence straight off $A = LL^{\top}$. Matching the $(i,j)$
entry for $i \ge j$ gives $A_{ij} = \sum_{k\le j} L_{ik}L_{jk}$. Solve for the
unknown $L$ entries one column at a time — the diagonal first,
$$
L_{jj} = \sqrt{A_{jj} - \textstyle\sum_{k<j} L_{jk}^2},
\qquad
L_{ij} = \frac{1}{L_{jj}}\Big(A_{ij} - \textstyle\sum_{k<j} L_{ik}L_{jk}\Big)
\;\;(i>j).
$$
Read it as: the diagonal entry is what is "left over" of $A_{jj}$ after removing
the part already explained by earlier columns, square-rooted; each below-diagonal
entry is the corresponding leftover, scaled by that diagonal. This is the
**Cholesky–Banachiewicz** order, and it is exactly what `nitrix` implements (see
`spd_chol` in `linalg/_smalllinalg.py`). One implementation nicety worth flagging
because it looks like magic: the source computes the inner sum as the *full* dot
product `L @ L[j]` rather than masking out $k<j$ — and that is correct precisely
because the not-yet-filled entries of $L$ are still zero, so the dot product
automatically restricts to the $k<j$ terms.

With $L$ in hand, solving $A\beta = c$ is two triangular sweeps:

$$
L\,w = c \;\;(\text{forward substitution}),\qquad
L^{\top}\beta = w \;\;(\text{back substitution}).
$$ {#eq-trisolve}

Forward substitution solves for $w$ top-to-bottom (the first row gives $w_1$
immediately, the second row uses $w_1$ to give $w_2$, and so on); back
substitution solves for $\beta$ bottom-to-top. Each sweep is $O(p^2)$ and dead
simple. `nitrix` does both at once through a single `triangular_solve`
(cuBLAS `trsm`, which works on the broken stack) against the identity to get the
inverse when it needs the full coefficient covariance, or against $c$ when it only
needs $\beta$.

### The log-determinant falls out for free

Here is the payoff that makes Cholesky the right factorisation for *statistics*,
not just for solving. Every likelihood you will meet in this suite contains a term
$\log\det V$ or $\log\det A$ — the "volume" term of a Gaussian (see
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-variance)). Determinants are numerically nasty:
$\det A$ is a product of $p$ numbers that can underflow to $0$ or overflow to
$\infty$ even when $A$ is perfectly well-behaved. But once you have $A = LL^{\top}$,
the determinant is

$$
\det A = \det(L)\det(L^{\top}) = \Big(\prod_i L_{ii}\Big)^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\log\det A = 2\sum_i \log L_{ii}.
$$ {#eq-logdet}

A *sum of logs of the diagonal* — computed in the safe additive regime, no
overflow, no separate work. This is the deep reason the whole suite carries
$\log\det$ rather than $\det$ everywhere: not just because the log is the form the
likelihood wants, but because Cholesky hands it to you for free and in the stable
representation. Every REML and IRLS objective in the later chapters reads its
$\log\det$ off a Cholesky diagonal exactly this way.

### `small_inv_logdet`: the shipped routine

The function the whole suite calls for the small dense solve is
`small_inv_logdet(A, n)` in `linalg/_smalllinalg.py`. It returns *both* the inverse
(or solve) *and* the log-determinant in one pass — because, as we just saw, the
factorisation that gives one gives the other essentially for free, and almost
every caller needs both (the coefficient covariance *and* the likelihood's volume
term). It is **size-dispatched** on the compile-time integer `n`:

- **$n = 1$**: a reciprocal and a `log`. Nothing to factor.
- **$n = 2$**: the closed-form symmetric inverse
  $\frac{1}{\det}\begin{bmatrix} c & -b \\ -b & a \end{bmatrix}$ for
  $A=\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{bmatrix}$, with
  $\det = ac - b^2$ and $\log\det = \log(ac-b^2)$. These are the common small
  designs — an intercept and one covariate — so the closed form avoids any loop.
- **$n > 2$**: the hand-rolled Cholesky path above (`spd_inv_logdet_chol`):
  factor $A = LL^{\top}$, invert through `triangular_solve`, read $\log\det$ off
  the diagonal as @eq-logdet.

Two details from the source are worth understanding rather than skimming. First,
**the Cholesky loop is *rolled*, not unrolled** — it runs as a `lax.fori_loop`
over the $p$ columns rather than being flattened into $O(p^3)$ scalar operations
at trace time. The reason is compile time: an unrolled factor makes the XLA
compile cost grow *cubically* in $p$ (the source measured ~29 s to compile a
single $p=30$ inverse unrolled), whereas rolling the loop keeps the compiled graph
$O(p^2)$ — one loop body — so compile stays roughly flat (~0.4 s at $p=30$). The
runtime flop count is the same $O(p^3)$ either way; only the *graph* shrinks. At
the brain scale, where this kernel is `vmap`-ed across a million voxels and
differentiated, that compile-time blowup would be ruinous.

Second, the **modified-Cholesky pivot floor**. The matrix being factored is the
normal-equation matrix $X^{\top}WX$, whose condition number is the *square* of the
design's — so an ill-conditioned design squares into a near-singular pivot, and
the $A_{jj} - \sum_{k<j}L_{jk}^2$ under the square root can dip non-positive from
roundoff alone. Rather than emit a `NaN` (which would poison the whole batched
`vmap`), the code clamps that pivot to a small floor, `~1e2 × eps(dtype)` times the
diagonal scale. This keeps a degenerate solve *finite and regularised* rather than
broken. It cannot restore digits that roundoff already lost — which is why the
suite expects **float64** for ill-conditioned designs (in float32 the floor fires
far sooner). The honest framing the source uses: the floor is numerical insurance,
not a substitute for a well-posed design.

@fig-solvers-cholesky shows a small SPD matrix and its Cholesky factor side by
side, so you can see the triangular structure and check $LL^{\top}=A$ numerically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-cholesky
#| fig-cap: "A 5x5 SPD matrix A (left) and its lower-triangular Cholesky factor L (centre). The reconstruction LLᵀ (right) matches A to machine precision; note L is exactly zero above the diagonal."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Build a small SPD matrix: A = B Bᵀ + ridge, guaranteed positive-definite.
p = 5
B = rng.standard_normal((p, p))
A = B @ B.T + p * np.eye(p)

# Hand-rolled Cholesky (Cholesky–Banachiewicz), mirroring spd_chol's recurrence.
def chol(A):
    n = A.shape[0]
    L = np.zeros((n, n))
    for j in range(n):
        s = L[j, :j] @ L[j, :j]
        L[j, j] = np.sqrt(A[j, j] - s)
        for i in range(j + 1, n):
            L[i, j] = (A[i, j] - L[i, :j] @ L[j, :j]) / L[j, j]
    return L

L = chol(A)
recon = L @ L.T
max_err = np.max(np.abs(recon - A))
logdet_chol = 2.0 * np.sum(np.log(np.diag(L)))
logdet_ref = np.linalg.slogdet(A)[1]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9, 3))
for ax, M, title in zip(
    axes, (A, L, recon), ("A (SPD)", r"L (Cholesky)", r"$LL^\top$")
):
    im = ax.imshow(M, cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-np.max(np.abs(A)), vmax=np.max(np.abs(A)))
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xticks(range(p))
    ax.set_yticks(range(p))
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axes, shrink=0.7)
fig.suptitle(
    rf"$\max|LL^\top - A| = {max_err:.1e}$    "
    rf"$\log\det A$: chol $= {logdet_chol:.4f}$, ref $= {logdet_ref:.4f}$",
    y=1.02,
)
plt.show()
```

The reconstruction error sits at machine precision, and the two log-determinants
agree — the one read off the factor diagonal (@eq-logdet) and a reference. That
agreement is the whole point: one factorisation, both the solve and the volume
term.

## Newton, and how to trust it {#sec-newton}

The second recurring job is to pick a few scalars — log-variances in a
[mixed model](lme.qmd#sec-reml), smoothing parameters in a
[GAM](gam.qmd#sec-penalty), the cut-points of an ordinal model — by minimising a
small, smooth negative log-likelihood $f(\theta)$. Here $\theta$ is short (two to
a handful of components), $f$ is smooth, and we want the minimiser. The optimiser
that does this across the whole suite is `damped_newton` in `stats/_optimise.py`.
We build it up one rung at a time.

### Gradient descent, and why it crawls

The first thing anyone tries is **gradient descent**: the gradient $\nabla f$
points uphill, so step downhill,

$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha\,\nabla f(\theta).
$$ {#eq-gd}

It works, but it can be agonisingly slow. Picture the loss surface as a long
narrow valley — steep across, gently sloping along. The gradient points mostly
*across* the valley (the steep direction), so the iterate zig-zags from wall to
wall while creeping only slowly toward the minimum at the far end. The technical
name for "long narrow valley" is **ill-conditioning** (the Hessian's eigenvalues
span a wide range), and gradient descent's step count grows with that spread. We
need a method that knows about the *shape* of the valley, not just its slope.

### Newton: use the curvature

The shape of the valley is exactly what the second derivative — the **Hessian**
$H = \nabla^2 f$ — encodes. Here is the idea, and it is one you could invent.
Near the current point, approximate $f$ by its second-order Taylor expansion: a
quadratic bowl. We know exactly where a quadratic is minimised — set its gradient
to zero. So *jump straight to the bottom of the local quadratic*. Expanding
$f(\theta + \delta) \approx f(\theta) + \nabla^{\top}\delta +
\tfrac12 \delta^{\top} H \delta$ and minimising over $\delta$ gives
$H\delta = -\nabla$, i.e.

$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - H^{-1}\nabla f(\theta).
$$ {#eq-newton}

This is **Newton's method**. Notice what $H^{-1}$ does: it rescales the gradient
by the curvature, stretching the step out along the gently-sloping floor of the
valley and shrinking it across the steep walls — precisely the correction gradient
descent lacked. If $f$ *is* a quadratic, one Newton step lands exactly on the
minimum. For a smooth $f$ near its optimum, convergence is quadratic — the number
of correct digits roughly doubles each step. (Solving $H\delta = -\nabla$ is, of
course, the small dense SPD solve of [§2](#sec-cholesky) — when $H$ is SPD.)

### Fisher scoring is IRLS

There is a beautiful special case worth naming because it ties this section to the
[GLM chapter](glm.qmd#sec-irls). For a GLM, the Hessian of the log-likelihood is
random (it depends on the data through second derivatives), but its *expectation*
— the **Fisher information** — has a clean closed form. Replacing $H$ by its
expectation $\mathcal{I}$ in @eq-newton is called **Fisher scoring**, and for a
GLM the resulting update is *exactly* iteratively-reweighted least squares:

$$
\beta \leftarrow \beta + \mathcal{I}^{-1}\,\nabla\ell
\;=\;(X^{\top}WX)^{-1}X^{\top}W z,
$$

the weighted normal equations @eq-normal with the IRLS weights $W$ and working
response $z$. So "Fisher scoring on a GLM" and "IRLS" are two names for one
algorithm — Newton's idea with the expected curvature substituted in. The suite's
IRLS core (`_irls.py`, `fit_penalised_irls`) is this loop, run for a fixed number
of iterations, each iteration a `small_inv_logdet` solve of @eq-normal.

### The overshoot problem, and damping

Newton's promise — one step to the bottom — holds for a *quadratic*. Real
likelihoods are not quadratic, and far from the optimum the local quadratic
approximation can be poor or even *wrong-way-up*. Two failures bite:

1. **Overshoot.** A full Newton step from a bad start can leap clear past the
   minimum and land somewhere the objective is *worse* than where it began.
2. **Indefinite curvature.** Away from the optimum $H$ can be indefinite (it has
   negative eigenvalues — a saddle). Then $-H^{-1}\nabla$ is not even a *descent*
   direction; it can point uphill, and the iterate stalls or diverges. The REML
   objective in particular is non-convex in the log-Cholesky parameterisation and
   is riddled with saddles away from the optimum (this is flagged explicitly in
   `_optimise.py`).

The cure is **damping**, and there are two equivalent ways to picture it.

*View A — step control (line search).* Take the Newton *direction* but do not
trust its *length*. Try the full step; if it does not decrease $f$, halve it, and
halve again, until it does. This is **backtracking line search**, and it
guarantees you never make things worse. `nitrix` implements exactly this in
`_backtrack`: try scales $1, \tfrac12, \tfrac14, \dots$ for a fixed number of
halvings and keep the best (if none improves, keep $\theta$ unchanged).

*View B — ridge on the curvature (Levenberg).* Add a multiple of the identity to
the Hessian before solving:

$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - (H + \lambda I)^{-1}\nabla f.
$$ {#eq-levenberg}

Read the two limits and the whole method falls out. When $\lambda$ is **large**,
$H+\lambda I \approx \lambda I$ and the step becomes $-\tfrac1\lambda\nabla$ — a
short, safe gradient-descent step. When $\lambda$ is **small**, it is the full
Newton step @eq-newton. So $\lambda$ dials continuously between "cautious
gradient" far away and "aggressive Newton" near the optimum. It doubles as
numerical insurance: a positive $\lambda$ keeps $H+\lambda I$ invertible even when
$H$ itself is near-singular. This is the **Levenberg** step, and `_optimise.py`
offers it as `step='damped'` (solved through `small_inv_logdet`) for the cases
where $H$ is PSD by construction — for instance the **average-information** REML
curvature, which is PSD by design (see [§4](#sec-woodbury) and the LME chapter).

For the saddle-prone case the source goes one better. Its default,
`step='saddle_free'`, eigendecomposes $H$ (via the cuSOLVER-free Jacobi routine of
the next paragraph) and replaces each eigenvalue $\mu_i$ by $|\mu_i|$ (floored).
Flipping the sign of the negative eigenvalues turns a saddle into a bowl while
keeping the directions $H$ identified, so the step is *guaranteed* to be a descent
direction even at an indefinite Hessian (Dauphin et al. 2014). The eigendecomposition
runs under `stop_gradient` because it only shapes the step, not the value.

That eigendecomposition is itself hand-rolled, for the same cuSOLVER reason as
Cholesky: `sym_eig_jacobi` in `linalg/_smalllinalg.py` is a **cyclic Jacobi**
eigensolver — a fixed number of sweeps of plane (Givens) rotations, each zeroing
one off-diagonal entry, applied as $A \leftarrow J^{\top} A J$. It is pure
arithmetic (no cuSOLVER `syevd`), `vmap`-clean, and converges to machine precision
in a handful of sweeps for the tiny matrices it serves (a contrast covariance or a
$\le 7$-component Hessian). It is *forward-only* — differentiating an iterative
eigensolver overflows as the off-diagonals vanish — which is fine because its
consumers run under `stop_gradient`.

### What `damped_newton` actually does

Putting the rungs together, `damped_newton(nll, theta0, *, n_iter, damping,
max_step, n_backtrack, curvature, step)` runs a **fixed** number of iterations
(`n_iter`), each one:

1. evaluate the gradient and curvature at $\theta$ — either by autodiff
   (`curvature=None` uses `jax.grad` + `jax.hessian` of `nll`) or by an
   **analytic** callback the solver supplies (e.g. the closed-form REML score and
   average-information matrix; this is the fork the block-Woodbury and FaST-LMM
   engines take, avoiding a second-order autodiff through a Cholesky);
2. form the step direction by the chosen rule (`saddle_free` or `damped`),
   **clipping** each component to `max_step` as a final overshoot guard;
3. backtrack (`n_backtrack` halvings) along that direction, keeping the best.

The *fixed* iteration budget is the crucial design choice for this codebase: a
`while`-loop with a convergence test branches on data, which breaks clean `vmap` /
`jit` across the image. A fixed `lax.scan` of, say, 20 steps is branch-free, so
the identical compiled program runs at every voxel — and the damping + backtracking
ensure those 20 steps are monotone and safe rather than merely numerous. This is
the single source of truth for the optimiser; the chapters that estimate variance
components all call into it.

@fig-solvers-newton contrasts a raw Newton step overshooting a non-quadratic valley
with a damped step converging cleanly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-newton
#| fig-cap: "A non-quadratic loss surface (contours), minimum at (1,1). From a poor start the raw Newton trajectory (red) trusts its local quadratic model, takes a step far too long, and flies out of the frame — it diverges. The damped trajectory (black) takes the saddle-free direction but backtracks any step that would not decrease the loss, walking down the valley floor to the minimum (star)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A curved valley with minimum at (1, 1), wrapped in log1p so that FAR from the
# optimum the quadratic (Newton) model is badly wrong -- the regime where a full
# Newton step overshoots. Same idea as Rosenbrock, gentler tails.
def base(p):
    x, y = p
    return (1.0 - x) ** 2 + 10.0 * (y - x * x) ** 2

def f(p):
    return np.log1p(base(p))

def grad(p):
    x, y = p
    gb = np.array([
        -2.0 * (1.0 - x) - 40.0 * x * (y - x * x),
        20.0 * (y - x * x),
    ])
    return gb / (1.0 + base(p))

def hess(p):
    x, y = p
    b = base(p)
    gb = np.array([
        -2.0 * (1.0 - x) - 40.0 * x * (y - x * x),
        20.0 * (y - x * x),
    ])
    Hb = np.array([
        [2.0 - 40.0 * (y - 3.0 * x * x), -40.0 * x],
        [-40.0 * x, 20.0],
    ])
    return Hb / (1.0 + b) - np.outer(gb, gb) / (1.0 + b) ** 2

def raw_step(p):
    # Full Newton: trust the local quadratic completely.
    return p - np.linalg.solve(hess(p), grad(p))

def damped_step(p):
    # Saddle-free curvature (|eigenvalues|, floored -> a guaranteed descent
    # direction even at an indefinite Hessian) plus a backtracking line search:
    # the same direction, never lengthened past where the loss decreases.
    H = hess(p)
    g = grad(p)
    w, Q = np.linalg.eigh(H)
    H_psd = Q @ np.diag(np.maximum(np.abs(w), 1e-2)) @ Q.T
    delta = np.linalg.solve(H_psd, g)
    scale = 1.0
    for _ in range(40):
        trial = p - scale * delta
        if f(trial) < f(p):
            return trial
        scale *= 0.5
    return p

start = np.array([-1.0, 1.5])
raw = [start.copy()]
dmp = [start.copy()]
for _ in range(12):
    raw.append(raw_step(raw[-1]))
    dmp.append(damped_step(dmp[-1]))
raw = np.array(raw)
dmp = np.array(dmp)

xs = np.linspace(-2.0, 2.0, 320)
ys = np.linspace(-1.5, 2.5, 320)
XX, YY = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
ZZ = np.log1p((1.0 - XX) ** 2 + 10.0 * (YY - XX * XX) ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4.5))
ax.contour(XX, YY, ZZ, levels=18, cmap="Greys", linewidths=0.6)
# Clip the raw trajectory to the window so the divergence reads as a line
# leaving the frame rather than rescaling the whole plot.
rc = raw.copy()
rc[:, 0] = np.clip(rc[:, 0], -2.0, 2.0)
rc[:, 1] = np.clip(rc[:, 1], -1.5, 2.5)
ax.plot(rc[:, 0], rc[:, 1], "-o", color="crimson", ms=3, lw=1.2,
        label="raw Newton (diverges)")
ax.annotate("raw step\nflies off", xy=rc[2], xytext=(-1.9, -1.2),
            color="crimson", fontsize=8,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="crimson", lw=1.0))
ax.plot(dmp[:, 0], dmp[:, 1], "-o", color="black", ms=3, lw=1.2,
        label="damped + backtrack")
ax.plot(1.0, 1.0, "*", color="gold", ms=18, mec="k", label="minimum")
ax.plot(*start, "s", color="steelblue", ms=8, label="start")
ax.set_xlim(-2.0, 2.0)
ax.set_ylim(-1.5, 2.5)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\theta_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\theta_2$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Raw vs damped Newton on a curved valley")
plt.show()
```

The raw step trusts the local quadratic far from the optimum, takes a wildly
overlong step, and leaves the basin entirely; the damped step takes the same
*direction* but never lets the loss go up, so it tracks the valley floor down to
the minimum. That contrast — direction from the curvature, length from a line
search — is the whole content of damping.

## Inverting a giant covariance cheaply {#sec-woodbury}

The third job appears the moment there are random effects. A
[mixed model](lme.qmd) has marginal (observation) covariance

$$
V_y = Z G Z^{\top} + R,
$$ {#eq-marg-cov}

where $Z$ is the $N\times q$ random-effects design, $G$ the $q\times q$
random-effect covariance, and $R$ the residual covariance (often $\sigma_e^2 I_N$).
Every REML likelihood needs $V_y^{-1}$ and $\log\det V_y$ — but $V_y$ is
$N\times N$. At $N$ in the hundreds, per voxel, across a million voxels, forming
and factoring it ($O(N^3)$ each) is hopeless. We must never build it.

### The Woodbury idea

Here is the intuition before any formula. $R$ is cheap to invert — it is diagonal.
The expensive part, $ZGZ^{\top}$, is a *low-rank* bump on top of $R$: it has rank
at most $q$, and $q$ is small (a few random effects, or a few parameters per
group). So we are asking: *if I already know the inverse of the cheap part $R$, how
much does a low-rank bump change it?* Surely not by much work — a rank-$q$ change
to a matrix ought to cost something like $q$, not $N$. The **Woodbury identity**
makes that precise:

$$
(R + Z G Z^{\top})^{-1}
= R^{-1} - R^{-1} Z\,(G^{-1} + Z^{\top} R^{-1} Z)^{-1} Z^{\top} R^{-1}.
$$ {#eq-woodbury}

Read it as a *correction*. Start from the cheap inverse $R^{-1}$. Then subtract a
correction that lives entirely in the $q$-dimensional random-effect space: the
only matrix you actually invert is the **capacitance matrix**
$K = G^{-1} + Z^{\top} R^{-1} Z$, which is just $q\times q$. Everything else is a
matrix multiply by $Z$ or $R^{-1}$. The cost collapses from $O(N^3)$ to roughly
$O(q^3 + Nq)$ — the $q^3$ to invert $K$, the $Nq$ for the multiplies. When $q \ll
N$, that is the difference between feasible and not. The matching determinant
identity (so $\log\det V_y$ is also cheap) is the matrix determinant lemma,
$\log\det V_y = \log\det R + \log\det G + \log\det K$.

This single identity is the workhorse behind every mixed-model and GLMM fit in the
suite. The [GLMM chapter](glmm.qmd) calls it the block-Woodbury structure; the LME
chapter uses it for correlated random slopes.

### Block-Woodbury: many groups stay cheap

Now the refinement `nitrix` actually ships, in `lme/_blockwoodbury.py`. The
generic Woodbury inverts one $q\times q$ capacitance. But in a grouped design — the
typical case, one random intercept-and-slope *per subject* — the random-effect
covariance $G$ and the design $Z$ have **block structure**: observations in
different groups share no random effects, so $V_y$ is **block-diagonal across
groups**. The marginal covariance is

$$
V_y = \operatorname{blockdiag}_i\big(\sigma_e^2 I_{n_i} + Z_i G Z_i^{\top}\big),
$$

one block per group $i$ (group $i$ has $n_i$ rows and its own $n_i\times r$
random-covariate block $Z_i$, where $r$ is the per-group random dimension — e.g.
$r=2$ for a random intercept and slope). A block-diagonal matrix inverts
block-by-block, so Woodbury applies *independently within each group*. Define the
per-group capacitance

$$
K_i = \sigma_e^2 G^{-1} + Z_i^{\top} Z_i \quad (r\times r),
$$ {#eq-blockwoodbury}

and the whole REML objective assembles from per-group $r\times r$ reductions plus
one $p\times p$ fixed-effect solve. The source spells out exactly the two
quantities every likelihood needs, per group:

$$
X_i^{\top} V_i^{-1} X_i
= \sigma_e^{-2}\big(X_i^{\top}X_i - (X_i^{\top}Z_i)\,K_i^{-1}\,(Z_i^{\top}X_i)\big),
\qquad
\log|V_i| = (n_i - r)\log\sigma_e^2 + \log|G| + \log|K_i|.
$$

The accounting is the point: every matrix that gets inverted is $r\times r$ (with
$r \le 3$) or $p\times p$ — never $N\times N$, never even $n_i\times n_i$. Adding
*more groups* adds more independent $r\times r$ blocks, so the cost grows linearly
in the number of groups rather than cubically in $N$. The connection to the
**Schur complement**: $K_i$ is exactly the Schur complement that arises when you
eliminate the fixed effects from the joint (mixed-model) equations of a group —
"correcting" the within-group Gram for what the random effects explain. The
function `group_grams` precomputes the data-independent per-group Grams
($Z_i^{\top}Z_i$, $X_i^{\top}Z_i$, $X_i^{\top}X_i$) once and shares them across all
voxels; `bw_score_and_ai` then assembles the analytic REML score and
average-information curvature from them — feeding the **analytic-curvature** fork of
`damped_newton` from [§3](#sec-newton), with `step='damped'` because that AI
curvature is PSD by construction.

@fig-solvers-woodbury shows the block structure of $ZZ^{\top}$ for grouped data —
the sparsity Woodbury exploits.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-woodbury
#| fig-cap: "Left: the random-effects design Z for 6 groups (one random intercept + slope per group), observations sorted by group. Right: ZZᵀ — block-diagonal across groups, so V = ZGZᵀ + R inverts one small block at a time rather than as one NxN matrix."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

n_groups = 6
per_group = 5
r = 2  # random intercept + slope
N = n_groups * per_group

# Block-structured Z: each group's rows load only on that group's r columns.
Z = np.zeros((N, n_groups * r))
group = np.repeat(np.arange(n_groups), per_group)
for i in range(N):
    g = group[i]
    x = rng.uniform(-1, 1)  # the random-slope covariate
    Z[i, g * r + 0] = 1.0       # intercept
    Z[i, g * r + 1] = x         # slope
ZZt = Z @ Z.T

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.5, 4))
axes[0].imshow(Z, cmap="viridis", aspect="auto")
axes[0].set_title(r"$Z$  ($N\times qr$)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("random-effect column")
axes[0].set_ylabel("observation (sorted by group)")
im = axes[1].imshow(ZZt, cmap="magma")
axes[1].set_title(r"$ZZ^\top$ — block-diagonal across groups")
axes[1].set_xlabel("observation")
axes[1].set_ylabel("observation")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1], shrink=0.8)
# Draw the group block boundaries.
for b in range(1, n_groups):
    axes[1].axhline(b * per_group - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.6)
    axes[1].axvline(b * per_group - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.6)
plt.show()
```

The off-diagonal blocks of $ZZ^{\top}$ are exactly zero: an observation in group 1
shares no random effect with one in group 4. That sparsity is what lets the
per-group Woodbury treat each block in isolation, so the whole inverse never has to
see $N$ at once.

## Diagonalising once: the spectral trick {#sec-spectral}

The fourth job is the most elegant, and it is what makes the *scalar* random-effect
case (one variance per subject) blazingly fast at brain scale. It lives in
`lme/reml.py` and is the **FaST-LMM** trick of Lippert et al. (2011).

### The setup that makes it possible

Specialise [the marginal covariance](#eq-marg-cov) to a single scalar random effect
with $R = \sigma_e^2 I$:

$$
V = \sigma_b^2\, Z Z^{\top} + \sigma_e^2 I_N.
$$

The shape $ZZ^{\top}$ is **fixed** — it does not change as we search over the
variances. Only the two scalars $\sigma_b^2, \sigma_e^2$ move. That is the
structure to exploit: when the only unknowns are scalar multipliers of a fixed
matrix, we should pay the cost of understanding that matrix's geometry *once* and
then ride it for free at every trial value.

### One eigendecomposition rotates the problem diagonal

The geometry of a symmetric matrix is its eigendecomposition. Write
$ZZ^{\top} = U \Lambda U^{\top}$ with $U$ orthonormal and $\Lambda =
\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_N)$. Rotate the data and design into
that eigenbasis: $\tilde y = U^{\top} y$, $\tilde X = U^{\top} X$. Because $U$ is
orthonormal, $U^{\top} I U = I$, so in the rotated coordinates the *whole*
covariance becomes **diagonal**:

$$
\tilde V = U^{\top} V U
= \sigma_b^2 \Lambda + \sigma_e^2 I
= \operatorname{diag}(d), \qquad
d_i = \sigma_b^2 \lambda_i + \sigma_e^2.
$$ {#eq-spectral}

Now look at what every expensive operation in the REML objective becomes. The
profile REML negative log-likelihood (derived in [the LME chapter](lme.qmd#sec-reml))
is

$$
\mathrm{nll}(\theta) = \tfrac12\Big[\log\det V
+ \log\det(X^{\top} V^{-1} X) + r^{\top} V^{-1} r\Big],
$$

and in the rotated, diagonal coordinates each piece collapses to a **sum over
eigenvalues**:

$$
\log\det V = \sum_i \log d_i,\qquad
V^{-1} = \operatorname{diag}(1/d_i),\qquad
\tilde X^{\top} V^{-1}\tilde X = \sum_i \frac{\tilde x_i \tilde x_i^{\top}}{d_i}.
$$

There is no matrix to factor any more. Each trial value of the variances costs
$O(N p^2 + N)$ — *elementwise* work on the vector $d$ — instead of an $O(N^3)$
refactorisation of $V$. The Newton iteration of [§3](#sec-newton) over $\theta =
(\log\sigma_b^2, \log\sigma_e^2)$ runs entirely on the diagonal $d$.

### Computed once, reused across the whole image

The eigendecomposition $ZZ^{\top} = U\Lambda U^{\top}$ costs $O(N^3)$ — but it
depends only on the *design* $Z$, which is **shared across all voxels** (every
voxel has the same subjects and the same group structure; only $y$ differs). So it
is computed **once**, at the outer call, and $U, \Lambda$ are reused for every one
of the million per-voxel fits. The one-off $O(N^3)$ is amortised to nothing; the
per-voxel work is the cheap diagonal Newton. This is the single place the suite
*does* use a library eigendecomposition — through `safe_eigh`, which tries the
fast path and falls back to a CPU eig if cuSOLVER is unavailable — precisely
because it is outside the hot path and run once.

There is a further refinement in the source worth knowing: when the random-effect
design is *tall* ($q < N$, the brain-scale norm — many observations, few random
effects), `reml_fit(low_rank=True)` eigendecomposes the much smaller $q\times q$
matrix $Z^{\top}Z$ instead of the $N\times N$ matrix $ZZ^{\top}$, recovering the
range-space eigenvectors as $U_r = ZW/\sqrt{s}$ and handling the $N-q$ null
directions through cheap Gram aggregates. The dispatcher auto-selects this when
$q < N$. The two paths match to the iterative tolerance; the low-rank one is one to
two orders of magnitude cheaper to set up.

@fig-solvers-spectral profiles the REML objective over the variance ratio — the
clean 1-D curve the diagonalised objective produces, evaluated at a sweep of trial
values for almost no cost.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solvers-spectral
#| fig-cap: "Profile REML objective vs the log variance-ratio log(σ_b²/σ_e²), evaluated through the spectral form (a sum over the fixed ZZᵀ eigenvalues). Each trial value is an O(N) sweep over the diagonal d, not an O(N³) refactorisation; the optimum (star) is where the random-effect variance is identified."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Simulate one voxel of a random-intercept model: N subjects in M groups.
N, M, p = 40, 8, 2
group = np.repeat(np.arange(M), N // M)
Z = np.zeros((N, M))
Z[np.arange(N), group] = 1.0            # one random intercept per group
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(N), rng.standard_normal(N)])  # intercept + covariate
beta_true = np.array([1.0, 0.5])
sb2_true, se2_true = 1.5, 0.5
b = rng.standard_normal(M) * np.sqrt(sb2_true)
y = X @ beta_true + Z @ b + rng.standard_normal(N) * np.sqrt(se2_true)

# Shared eigendecomposition of ZZᵀ — computed ONCE.
ZZt = Z @ Z.T
lam, U = np.linalg.eigh(ZZt)
lam = np.clip(lam, 0.0, None)
y_rot = U.T @ y
X_rot = U.T @ X

def profile_reml_nll(log_ratio):
    # Profile out the overall scale: fix se2 = 1, sb2 = exp(log_ratio).
    sb2 = np.exp(log_ratio)
    d = sb2 * lam + 1.0                       # diagonal of rotated V
    Vinv_diag = 1.0 / d
    XtVinvX = (X_rot.T * Vinv_diag) @ X_rot   # p x p
    XtVinvy = (X_rot.T * Vinv_diag) @ y_rot
    beta = np.linalg.solve(XtVinvX, XtVinvy)
    r = y_rot - X_rot @ beta
    rss = np.sum(r * r * Vinv_diag)
    logdet_V = np.sum(np.log(d))
    logdet_XtVinvX = np.linalg.slogdet(XtVinvX)[1]
    return 0.5 * (logdet_V + logdet_XtVinvX + rss)

ratios = np.linspace(-4.0, 4.0, 200)
nll = np.array([profile_reml_nll(lr) for lr in ratios])
opt = ratios[np.argmin(nll)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.plot(ratios, nll, color="navy", lw=1.6)
ax.axvline(opt, color="crimson", ls="--", lw=1.0)
ax.plot(opt, nll.min(), "*", color="gold", ms=16, mec="k")
ax.axvline(np.log(sb2_true / se2_true), color="grey", ls=":", lw=1.0,
           label="true ratio")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\log(\sigma_b^2/\sigma_e^2)$")
ax.set_ylabel("profile REML negative log-likelihood")
ax.set_title("REML objective via the spectral (FaST-LMM) form")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
plt.show()
```

The curve is smooth and unimodal, with its minimum near the true variance ratio —
and every one of its 200 points cost an $O(N)$ diagonal sweep, not a refactorisation.
That is the spectral trick in one picture: pay $O(N^3)$ once for the rotation, then
search the variances for almost free.

## Further reading

- Golub, G. H., & Van Loan, C. F. (2013). *Matrix Computations* (4th ed.). Johns
  Hopkins University Press. — The reference for the Cholesky factorisation,
  triangular solves, conditioning, and the Woodbury / Sherman–Morrison identities.
- Nocedal, J., & Wright, S. J. (2006). *Numerical Optimization* (2nd ed.).
  Springer. — Newton's method, line search and backtracking, Levenberg–Marquardt
  damping, and trust regions.
- Dauphin, Y. N., Pascanu, R., Gulcehre, C., Cho, K., Ganguli, S., & Bengio, Y.
  (2014). Identifying and attacking the saddle point problem in high-dimensional
  non-convex optimization. *NeurIPS*. — The saddle-free Newton modification (flip
  the sign of negative Hessian eigenvalues) used by `step='saddle_free'`.
- Henderson, C. R. (1975). Best linear unbiased estimation and prediction under a
  selection model. *Biometrics*, 31. — The mixed-model equations and the joint
  fixed/random system the Schur complement eliminates.
- Harville, D. A. (1977). Maximum likelihood approaches to variance component
  estimation and to related problems. *JASA*, 72. — REML and the variance-component
  likelihood computations.
- Lippert, C., Listgarten, J., Liu, Y., Kadie, C. M., Davidson, R. I., &
  Heckerman, D. (2011). FaST linear mixed models for genome-wide association
  studies. *Nature Methods*, 8. — The spectral diagonalisation trick of
  [§5](#sec-spectral).
- Lindstrom, M. J., & Bates, D. M. (1990). Newton–Raphson and EM algorithms for
  linear mixed-effects models. *JASA*, 83. — Newton-scoring for variance components.

## See also

Source for the routines on this page (GitHub):

- [`linalg/_smalllinalg.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_smalllinalg.py)
  — `small_inv_logdet`, `spd_chol`, `spd_inv_logdet_chol`, `sym_eig_jacobi` (the
  cuSOLVER-free small dense linear algebra).
- [`stats/_optimise.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_optimise.py)
  — `damped_newton` (the shared damped/saddle-free Newton optimiser).
- [`stats/_irls.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/_irls.py)
  — `fit_penalised_irls` (Fisher scoring = IRLS).
- [`stats/lme/_blockwoodbury.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_blockwoodbury.py)
  — the per-group block-Woodbury REML.
- [`stats/lme/reml.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/reml.py)
  — `reml_fit` (the FaST-LMM spectral path).

Where these solvers are used:

- [GLM](glm.qmd#sec-irls) — IRLS is Fisher scoring; every step is the small dense
  solve of [§2](#sec-cholesky).
- [LME](lme.qmd#sec-reml) — REML by Newton ([§3](#sec-newton)) over variances,
  with the spectral trick of [§5](#sec-spectral).
- [GLMM](glmm.qmd) — the block-Woodbury structure of [§4](#sec-woodbury).
- [GAM](gam.qmd#sec-penalty) and [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd#the-update) —
  the penalised normal equations @eq-normal and the small Newton step.
